name: Build Your Own Print Library
scenario: You're building a console output library for your team's CLI tools. Engineers want consistent formatting across all the tools, so the team lead has asked you to build a `Display` utility class. It needs to handle a variety of inputs (numbers, strings, errors, success messages, banners) with a single naming convention. This is the same pattern System.out.println uses — but you're writing your own version. After this project, you'll understand exactly how the Java standard library is built.
description: Build a `Display` class with heavily overloaded `print` methods (and a few helper methods) that produce consistently formatted console output. The goal: a clean, intuitive API that engineers can learn in 30 seconds and use everywhere.

core.description: `printBanner(String title)` — prints title with === borders above and below
main() demonstrates EVERY method (proving overloads work)

bonus.description: Add `print(String... messages)` varargs version — prints each on its own line with [LIST] prefix
Add `printError(String message)`, `printSuccess(String message)`, `printWarning(String message)` with appropriate prefixes (✗ for error, ✓ for success, ⚠ for warning)
Add `printBanner(String title, char borderChar)` overload that uses a custom border character (#, *, etc.)
Add `printKeyValue(String key, String value)` and `printKeyValue(String key, int value)` overloads that align colons (advanced formatting)

data_to_use:
In main(), demonstrate each method. Use realistic values like:
- Messages: 'Server started', 'Connection established'
- Numbers: 42, 3.14, 1024
- Key-value pairs: ('CPU', 'Intel i7'), ('Memory', 16), ('Uptime', 99.7)
- Banner: 'SYSTEM STATUS'

criteria: Output is clean and consistent (5 pts)
Overloads do the same conceptual thing (5 pts)
Output formatting is consistent across overloads (5 pts)
API feels intuitive to use (5 pts)
Banner with custom border char (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. Overloading is one of the easier topics syntactically — the challenge is DESIGN. Think about how your library will be used. Would YOU enjoy using this API? Plan the method signatures BEFORE coding.
submission_instructions: Save as Display.java. Run it. Look at the output — does it feel like a real, professional library? Try renaming all the overloads with unique names (printMessage, printInt, etc.). Notice how much WORSE it feels. That's the value of overloading. Commit to your portfolio.
tips_for_success: Design ALL signatures first on paper. Then implement. This prevents 'fixing' overloads mid-build.
Notice: print(String, int) and print(String, double) are valid overloads (different types in 2nd position).
Avoid duplication: if banner border logic is in multiple places, extract a helper method.
Test each overload INDIVIDUALLY by calling it from main, then ADD MORE — incremental verification.
For the bonus aligned formatting: String.format("%-15s : %s", key, value) gives left-padded keys.
Compare your library to System.out.println's overloads (Java docs) — see how the pros do it.
concepts_reinforced: Method overloading with various parameter types and counts
Choosing parameter ORDER thoughtfully (label first vs. value first)
API design: making libraries easy to use
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Beyond syntax, you learn API DESIGN — a senior-engineer skill. Every overload is a choice: which parameter order? Should the boolean be a separate method or a flag? Are there too many overloads? These choices, multiplied across the methods in real libraries (Spring has thousands of methods), shape how usable a library is. Bad API design feels like fighting your tools; good API design feels invisible. Your Display class is a tiny taste of API design — the same skill that makes someone valuable at any senior position.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add color support using ANSI escape codes (e.g., printError uses red)
Add a verbosity level: print(int level, String message) — only prints if level >= currentVerbosity
Add print(String message, Object... args) like printf — formatted strings with placeholders
Convert to instance class (preview of OOP) with state like 'currentLogLevel'
Add file output: every print also goes to a log file
